items.prompt_md: After sorting intervals by **start**, what decides whether the current interval merges into the last interval in the result list?
options: Whether the current interval's start is at or before the last merged interval's end
Whether the current interval is longer than the last merged interval
Whether the current interval's end is at or before the last merged interval's start
Whether the two intervals share the same start value
items.answer: Whether the current interval's start is at or before the last merged interval's end
items.explanation_md: Sorting by start means the current interval's start is always the largest seen so far. If that start still falls inside the last merged interval's span (start <= its end), the two overlap and should fuse; otherwise the last merged interval is done and a new one begins.

items.prompt_md: You're given a list of intervals, some overlapping, and must return the minimal set of intervals that covers the same ranges. Which approach fits in a single pass after preprocessing?
options: Sort by start, then merge each interval into the last one whenever it overlaps
Compare every pair of intervals with nested loops and merge on the fly
Sort by end, then binary search for the next non-overlapping interval
Build a hash set of every covered point and scan for boundaries
items.answer: Sort by start, then merge each interval into the last one whenever it overlaps
items.explanation_md: Sorting by start guarantees any interval that can still overlap the one you're building sits immediately next in order, so one running "current merged interval" plus a single sweep is enough — no nested comparison and no per-point hashing needed.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the sort-and-sweep merge over n intervals?
items.explanation_md: The sweep itself is a single O(n) pass, but sorting the intervals by start dominates at O(n log n), so that's the overall bound.
